risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random numbers method for selecting participants, and the study also states that participants were "randomly assigned to one of the two groups". This is sufficient to judge that a proper random allocation sequence was used.

Quote: "Written consent was obtained in a face-to-face interview and the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group)." Comment: This explicitly describes a sealed opaque envelope used for allocation, which is a concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment. Although the review notes only "a sealed opaque envelope" rather than the full SNOSE wording, the paper clearly indicates the envelope was opaque and sealed at the point of assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants ... were then randomly assigned to one of the two groups in this study: psychoeducation and mutual support group (n = 34) or standard care (n = 34)." and "Participants (n = 34) received a 36-week program..."; "Routine Care... received the conventional psychiatric outpatient and family services." Comment: No blinding of participants or intervention-delivering personnel is described, and the intervention was clearly distinguishable from routine care. Because the main outcomes included caregiver burden, family functioning, service use, and patient functioning—outcomes plausibly influenced by awareness of allocation and group interactions—performance bias is likely.

Quote: "An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by an independent assessor rather than the treating staff. The main questionnaire outcomes (FBIS, FAD, FSSI, SLOF) and rehospitalisation data from records were collected by this assessor/records, so the primary measured outcomes were either assessed independently or obtained from hospital/outpatient record systems. Knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced these measurements in a way that would materially bias the outcomes, especially for the record-based rehospitalisation outcome; therefore detection bias is low.

Quote: "Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis [35]. All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period." Comment: The paper reports ITT analysis and follow-up of all randomized participants regardless of completion, and the attrition appears very low (31/34 completed in the intervention arm; dropout/discontinuation noted for 3 intervention and 2 control participants, with all 68 evaluated at outcome time points). Reasons for dropout were described and were not obviously differential or clearly outcome-related overall, so bias from missing data is unlikely.
